examples.title: Subset Sum using Dynamic Programming
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SubsetSumDP(arr[1..n], W)
BEGIN
  // Create DP table
  DECLARE dp[0..n][0..W] as BOOLEAN
  
  // Initialize base cases
  FOR j FROM 0 TO W DO
    dp[0][j] = FALSE
  END FOR
  dp[0][0] = TRUE
  
  // Fill DP table
  FOR i FROM 1 TO n DO
    FOR j FROM 0 TO W DO
      dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j]  // Not include arr[i]
      
      IF j >= arr[i] THEN
        IF dp[i-1][j - arr[i]] = TRUE THEN
          dp[i][j] = TRUE
        END IF
      END IF
    END FOR
  END FOR
  
  RETURN dp[n][W]
END
examples.explanation: DP table stores whether sum j is achievable using first i elements. Base case: empty set can make sum 0. For each element, either don't include it or include it if it doesn't exceed target.

examples.title: Subset Sum with Space Optimization
examples.code:
def subset_sum_dp(arr, target):
    n = len(arr)
    
    # Space optimized: 1D array
    dp = [False] * (target + 1)
    dp[0] = True  # Base case: sum 0 achievable
    
    for i in range(n):
        # Iterate backwards to avoid using current element twice
        for j in range(target, arr[i] - 1, -1):
            dp[j] = dp[j] or dp[j - arr[i]]
    
    return dp[target]

# Example usage
arr = [3, 34, 4, 12, 5, 2]
target = 9
print(subset_sum_dp(arr, target))  # Output: True
examples.explanation: Using 1D array and iterating backwards ensures each element is used at most once. When iterating backwards, dp[j-arr[i]] refers to the previous row's value, not the current row's updated value.

examples.title: Subset Sum using Backtracking
examples.explanation: Recursive function tries including and excluding each element. Pruning stops when sum exceeds target or remaining elements cannot reach target. Time is O(2^n) worst case but pruning helps significantly.

examples.title: Meet-in-the-Middle Subset Sum
examples.code:
def meet_in_the_middle(arr, target):
    n = len(arr)
    mid = n // 2
    
    # Split into two halves
    left = arr[:mid]
    right = arr[mid:]
    
    # Generate all subset sums for each half
    left_sums = [0]
    for num in left:
        left_sums += [s + num for s in left_sums]
    
    right_sums = [0]
    for num in right:
        right_sums += [s + num for s in right_sums]
    
    # Sort one list for binary search
    right_sums.sort()
    
    # Check if any pair sums to target
    for ls in left_sums:
        if target - ls in right_sums:
            return True
    
    return False

# Example
arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]
target = 15
print(meet_in_the_middle(arr, target))  # True (e.g., 7+8 or 1+3+5+6)
examples.explanation: Generates 2^(n/2) subset sums for each half, then searches for complementary pairs. Much faster than pure exponential for moderate n. Space is O(2^(n/2)).
